text.title: Cloud Provider Selection Decision Tree Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: ADR recommends two hyperscalers with no documented justification beyond 'avoid vendor lock-in'.
antipattern.root-cause: Treating multi-cloud as a best practice rather than a goal-driven choice.
antipattern.fix: Block ADR approval unless the rationale field names a specific goal (DR, regulatory, best-of-breed) and quantifies the operational overhead accepted.

antipattern.symptom: Microsoft-shop team is routed to AWS purely on workload-type weighting; identity-migration cost not assessed.
antipattern.fix: Reorder evaluation: r1 (investment) → r4 (compliance) → r2 (workload). Reject ADRs that skip r1.

antipattern.symptom: ADR picks GCP for a HIPAA-regulated workload before confirming GCP's BAA covers the chosen services.
antipattern.root-cause: Compliance matrix gathered after the decision instead of before it.
antipattern.fix: Compliance is a hard gate at step 1; any unmet requirement immediately eliminates the provider.

antipattern.symptom: TCO table compares compute + storage but omits egress and cross-AZ traffic; provider with cheapest list price loses by 40 % once egress is included.
antipattern.root-cause: Cost model copied from blog comparisons that exclude egress.
antipattern.fix: Mandate egress + cross-AZ rows in the cost model; reject TCO tables missing them.
